skill.name: gene-database
skill.description: Query the NCBI Gene database via E-utilities and the NCBI Datasets API; use it when you need to search genes by symbol/ID and retrieve annotations (RefSeq, GO, location, phenotype) for single or batch gene lists.
skill.summary: Retrieve gene annotations from the NCBI Gene database by symbol, ID, or disease term using E-utilities and Datasets API.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer flagged 358 potential issues, but evaluation confirms all high and medium findings are false positives. External command detections come from markdown documentation code blocks showing example CLI invocations, not from executable code. Network connections target legitimate NCBI public API endpoints (eutils.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov, api.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov). Environment variable references are optional API key parameters passed by the user, not hardcoded credentials. The only confirmed finding is file write access in batch_gene_lookup.py, which is user-initiated output. No malicious intent, obfuscation, or prompt injection detected.

medium_findings.title: Dangerous pattern combination detected by static heuristic
medium_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged a combination of network access, environment variable access, and filesystem write as a heuristic risk. Evaluation confirms this is a false positive: network calls go to NCBI public APIs only, environment variables are optional user-provided API keys, and file writes are user-directed output. This combination is inherent to legitimate bioinformatics API tooling.

low_findings.title: External commands in documentation files
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged shell backtick patterns in markdown documentation files (SKILL.md, references/api_reference.md, references/common_workflows.md). This is a false positive - these are example CLI commands inside markdown code blocks used for documentation purposes. No actual command execution occurs from these documentation files.

low_findings.title: Network access to NCBI public API endpoints
low_findings.description: The scripts make HTTP requests to NCBI public API endpoints (eutils.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov, api.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov). This is legitimate and intentional behavior for querying the NCBI Gene database. URLs are hardcoded base paths to public government research APIs, not to unknown or suspicious destinations.

low_findings.title: API key parameter passing pattern
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged api_key function parameters and --api-key CLI arguments as credential access. This is a false positive - these are optional parameters for passing an NCBI API key to increase rate limits. No hardcoded credentials exist in the codebase. The keys are provided by the user at invocation time.

low_findings.title: System reconnaissance in legacy audit JSON
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged system reconnaissance patterns in gene-database_audit_result_v1.json. This is a false positive. The file is a legacy audit result containing structured metadata about the skill's previous evaluation results, not reconnaissance or system scanning commands.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-directed file write in batch script
dangerous_patterns.description: batch_gene_lookup.py writes JSON output to a user-specified file path. The file path is provided via the --output CLI argument, so the write is explicitly user-initiated. The script validates the path via argparse. This is low risk as the user controls the destination, but it does provide filesystem write capability.

content.user_title: Search and retrieve gene data from the NCBI Gene database
content.value_statement: Researchers spend hours navigating the NCBI web interface to look up gene annotations for their work. This skill lets you search by gene symbol, NCBI Gene ID, or disease term and retrieve structured gene data directly through your AI tool.
actual_capabilities: Search genes by symbol or name with organism scoping using NCBI E-utilities
Retrieve gene records by NCBI Gene ID with annotations including RefSeq, GO terms, and genomic location
Batch lookup of multiple genes from a file or comma-separated list
Search genes by GO terms, phenotype keywords, or disease terms
Optional NCBI API key support for increased rate limits up to 10 requests per second
Output results in JSON, XML, or human-readable text format
limitations: Requires internet access to query the NCBI public API
Limited to gene data available in the NCBI Gene database
Subject to NCBI rate limits (3 requests per second without API key)
Cannot write to or modify data in the NCBI database

use_cases.title: Annotate a gene panel for publication
use_cases.description: Load a list of gene symbols from a file and batch retrieve standardized annotations including RefSeq accessions, GO terms, and genomic locations.

use_cases.title: Find disease-associated genes
use_cases.description: Search for genes linked to a specific disease or phenotype keyword, then retrieve detailed information for each result.

use_cases.title: Cross-species gene comparison
use_cases.description: Look up the same gene symbol across different organisms to compare gene IDs, descriptions, and genomic locations.

prompt_templates.title: Look up a single gene by symbol
prompt_templates.prompt: Search for the gene BRCA1 in humans and show me the NCBI Gene ID, description, genomic location, and known aliases.

prompt_templates.title: Annotate a list of genes from a file
prompt_templates.prompt: I have a file called my_genes.txt with gene symbols, one per line. Read the file and look up each gene in the NCBI database for human. Save the results to a JSON file called gene_annotations.json.

prompt_templates.title: Search by disease association
prompt_templates.prompt: Search the NCBI Gene database for genes associated with Alzheimer disease in humans. Retrieve the top 30 results with their descriptions and chromosomal locations.

prompt_templates.title: Retrieve detailed gene data with protein annotations
prompt_templates.prompt: Retrieve the full gene record for human TP53 (Gene ID 7157) using the NCBI Datasets API. Show me the gene description, synonyms, genomic ranges, and a list of known transcripts with their RefSeq accessions.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced user needs comprehensive gene metadata

output_examples.input: Look up the gene BRCA1 in humans
Search for genes associated with diabetes in humans
output: Found 25 genes associated with diabetes in humans
- ABCC8 (Gene ID: 6833) - ATP binding cassette subfamily C member 8

output_examples.input: Annotate my gene list from cancer_panel.txt for human
output: - TP53: Gene ID 7157, chr17:7,661,753-7,687,539, tumor suppressor
- EGFR: Gene ID 1956, chr7:55,019,017-55,211,628, receptor tyrosine kinase

best_practices: Always specify the organism when searching by gene symbol to avoid ambiguous results across different species.
Use an NCBI API key for batch lookups to increase rate limits from 3 to 10 requests per second.
Cache gene lookup results locally when processing large gene lists to reduce redundant API calls and stay within rate limits.
anti_patterns: Do not search by gene symbol alone without specifying the organism, as the same symbol can exist in multiple species.
Do not make rapid-fire API calls without rate limiting, as NCBI will return 429 Too Many Requests errors.
Do not rely on gene symbols as permanent identifiers, as symbols can change over time in the NCBI database.

faq.question: What databases does this skill access?
faq.answer: This skill queries the NCBI Gene database through the public E-utilities and NCBI Datasets APIs.

faq.question: Do I need an API key to use this skill?
faq.answer: No, but using a free NCBI API key increases the rate limit from 3 to 10 requests per second.

faq.question: Can I look up multiple genes at once?
faq.answer: Yes. You can pass a comma-separated list of Gene IDs or use a file with one gene symbol per line.

faq.question: What types of gene annotations can I retrieve?
faq.answer: You can retrieve gene symbol, description, genomic location, RefSeq transcripts, GO annotations, phenotypes, and literature references.

faq.question: Is this skill limited to human genes?
faq.answer: No. It supports any organism in the NCBI Gene database including mouse, rat, zebrafish, fruit fly, and many others.

faq.question: Can I use this skill to modify the NCBI database?
faq.answer: No. This skill is read-only. It searches and retrieves data from NCBI but cannot submit or modify records.
